medications.indication_rationale: First-line antifungal for seborrhoeic dermatitis; source used ketoconazole 2% cream to the face twice daily and 2% shampoo to the scalp (leave on ~5 minutes).
medications.dose_route_frequency: cream twice daily to affected areas; shampoo, leave ~5 minutes then rinse (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: A short (≤5-day) course of hydrocortisone 1% eases the acute facial burning/inflammation; low potency is appropriate on the face; source used it once daily for 5 days.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply sparingly once daily for ~5 days to the face (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: potent/prolonged topical steroid on the face
medications.indication_rationale: Potent or prolonged facial steroids cause skin atrophy, telangiectasia and perioral dermatitis — avoid on the face.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use a gentle pH-balanced cleanser (stop harsh alkaline soaps and scrubbing); leave the medicated shampoo on for ~5 minutes; consider a humidifier for dry heated air.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Harsh soaps/scrubbing worsen the flare; adequate shampoo contact time is needed.

patient_education_points.point: This is seborrhoeic dermatitis — your skin over-reacting to a normal yeast in oily areas. It's not lupus or a serious infection. Antifungal cream/shampoo plus a short course of a mild steroid settle it over days; it's a recurring condition that needs occasional maintenance.
Stop harsh soaps and scrubbing, use a gentle cleanser, and leave the medicated shampoo on for a few minutes before rinsing. Keep using antifungal shampoo intermittently to prevent flares.

See a doctor / go to ED if one area becomes hot, deeply red, swollen and painful with a fever (a spreading skin infection), or if you develop joint pains and a sun-worsened cheek rash that needs checking.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response; establish maintenance; specialist review if atypical/refractory or lupus suspected.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Facial cellulitis features — GP/ED; systemic/lupus features — GP.

must_include_items: Recognise seborrhoeic dermatitis (nasolabial-fold clue) vs lupus/cellulitis
Topical antifungal (cream + shampoo) + short low-potency facial steroid
Gentle skin care (stop harsh soaps/scrubbing) + maintenance
acceptable_alternatives: Ketoconazole or other azole/ciclopirox; a calcineurin inhibitor (e.g. pimecrolimus) as a steroid-sparing facial option
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires antifungal + short low-potency facial steroid with gentle skin care, and a facial-cellulitis/lupus safety-net.
